description: The GPIO module allows each pin to be configured as input or output using the DIR01 register. By default, all pins reset as inputs (bit value 1). Clearing the corresponding DIR bit configures a pin as an output, while setting it configures an input. Direction applies per pin across banks 0 and 1. This enables flexible per-signal role assignment in software.
dependencies: PSC clock enable; Pin multiplexing configured
Remarks: Update DIR before driving outputs; read back configuration to confirm.

description: GPIO outputs are driven using OUT_DATA01, SET_DATA01, and CLR_DATA01 registers. OUT supports read‑modify‑write to set complete output vectors. SET drives selected outputs high and CLR drives selected outputs low without affecting other bits. Reads of these registers return the programmed output state, not necessarily the actual pin if configured as input. Use IN_DATA01 to observe actual line level.
Remarks: Prefer SET/CLR for atomic bit updates when multiple contexts may access GPIO.

description: The current state of GPIO pins is read through IN_DATA01. For pins configured as inputs, the value is synchronized to the GPIO peripheral clock. For pins configured as outputs, reads return the device’s driven value. Some signals may be open-drain per device data manual, in which case IN reflects the wired-logic level. Synchronization avoids metastability when sampling asynchronous inputs.
Remarks: Debounce or filter external signals in software if required by the application.

description: Every GPIO pin can generate an interrupt on rising edges, falling edges, both edges, or be disabled. Edge detection is synchronized to the GPIO clock. Configuration is done via SET_RIS_TRIG01/CLR_RIS_TRIG01 and SET_FAL_TRIG01/CLR_FAL_TRIG01. Direction does not need to be input for interrupt generation, enabling software-driven test interrupts. Disable specific edges by writing to the corresponding CLR registers.
dependencies: BINTEN enable; System interrupt routing per device manual
Remarks: Clear pending conditions and configure edges before enabling CPU routing to avoid spurious IRQs.

description: Bank-level interrupt enables gate all GPIO pin interrupt sources per bank. Setting EN0 or EN1 in BINTEN enables events from banks 0 or 1 respectively. The same bits also control EDMA event generation. This provides coarse-grained enable for groups of 16 pins. Use with per-pin edge configuration for full control.
Remarks: Use bank enable as a fast mask during critical sections or low-power modes.

description: Pending GPIO interrupt events per pin are reported in INTSTAT01. A logic 1 indicates a pending interrupt and is cleared by writing a 1 to that bit (write-one-to-clear). Software must ensure all pending sources are serviced. For pins directly routed to the CPU, status may also be visible via CPU interrupt flags. Use status to disambiguate sources after a bank interrupt.
Remarks: Always read-modify-write carefully when clearing multiple bits to avoid race conditions.

description: GPIO can generate synchronization events to the EDMA for data movement orchestration. The same bank enable bits control availability of these events. This allows GPIO activity to trigger DMA transfers without CPU intervention. Event mapping details are device-specific and provided in the data manual. Use edge configuration to precisely define event timing.
dependencies: EDMA controller; BINTEN configuration; System event mapping
Remarks: Consult the device data manual for exact event numbers and routing to EDMA channels.

feature_name: Power and Clock Control via PSC
description: The GPIO peripheral clock is disabled by default and must be enabled via the local Power and Sleep Controller. The GPIO input clock runs at CPU/6 frequency. When placed in low-power state by the PSC, interrupt generation is suspended until re-enabled. Output states are maintained during low-power entry. Proper PSC sequencing is required during initialization and reset.
Remarks: Gate the GPIO clock when inactive to save power and re-enable before accessing registers.

description: The device uses extensive pin multiplexing to support many peripheral functions in limited package pins. GPIO function availability on specific pins depends on reset configuration and programmable pinmux registers. Software must configure pin multiplexing to select GPIO functionality where required. Details are device-specific and defined in the data manual. Incorrect pinmux prevents GPIO from controlling or sensing external pins.
dependencies: Device pinmux registers; Device data manual
Remarks: Configure pinmux prior to DIR and data settings and lock configuration if supported.

description: Hardware reset returns GPIO configuration and data registers to default states. Software reset via emulator does not modify GPIO signal configuration. A PSC reset cycle (clock disable, PSC reset, then clock enable) restores default register settings. After hard reset, all pins default to inputs. Software should reapply initialization to ensure correct operation.
Remarks: Design startup code to handle both emulator and hardware reset scenarios deterministically.

description: GPIO initialization requires configuring pin multiplexing, enabling the module through the PSC, and programming direction, data, and interrupt control registers. This sequence prepares the module for data transactions. Skipping pinmux or PSC enable prevents register access and pin control. Edge configuration should be set before enabling bank interrupts. Follow the documented order to avoid unintended behavior.
dependencies: Pinmux; PSC; DIR01; BINTEN; Edge trigger registers
Remarks: Apply initialization early in boot and encapsulate in a reusable driver routine.

description: GPIO signals are organized into two banks of 16 signals each. Control registers are 32-bit and operate per pair of banks with bank-specific bit fields. Register and field naming follow the pattern register_nameXY for banks and field_nameN for pin numbers. Table 1 provides mapping of bank and bit positions. This structure simplifies uniform access across all 32 pins.
Remarks: Use consistent bit masks derived from pin numbers to avoid bank addressing errors.

description: GPIO operation is independent of the processor endianness. There are no endianness-related considerations for the module. Register semantics and bit positions are unaffected by endianness. This simplifies software portability across endianness configurations. No special handling is required for byte ordering.
Remarks: Leverage the independence to share driver code across big- and little-endian builds.

description: The GPIO peripheral is not affected by emulation suspend events such as halts and breakpoints. This means GPIO hardware continues operating while the CPU is halted. Software must consider that external signals may change during debug stops. Interrupts and outputs will reflect ongoing hardware activity. Careful debug practices are necessary when interacting with real-time pins.
Remarks: Use GPIO cautiously during in-circuit debugging to prevent unintended system interactions.

description: The GPIO peripheral connects to external devices but by itself is not compliant to any specific bus or protocol standards. Software may implement standard connectivity protocols over GPIO if desired. There is no inherent protocol-layer enforcement in the hardware. This clarifies scope of interoperability expectations. External electrical characteristics follow device specifications.
Remarks: Ensure any bit-banged protocols meet timing and electrical requirements documented elsewhere.

feature_name: Output State Readback and Actual Pin State
description: Reading SET_DATA01, CLR_DATA01, and OUT_DATA01 returns the output state as programmed in registers. The actual physical pin level may differ if the pin is configured as input or is open-drain. To obtain the true signal level at the pad, software must read IN_DATA01. This distinction is critical for reliable state verification. Use appropriate register for intended observation.
Remarks: Avoid relying on OUT register readback for feedback in input or open-drain configurations.
